speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with William Stowell, better known as Michigan Bill, took place at his home in Lewiston on September 28th, 1976. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Place to place, place to place, job to job. Well, sure. That's a way to get there.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Sure, I've done it for years.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah. I used to hate to pay my fare. I don't know why, but I did anyway. Yeah, that was the crust of then.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: You know what I mean?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Everybody did it. That is lumberjack.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: It's, uh, it's summertime, but kind of cold in the winter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where would you usually wind up riding on the train? Where?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah, when you hopped in them days.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, if you were riding a freight, you'd get inside if you could in an empty barster. Otherwise, you're over outside. And the passenger train either went underneath or the blinds. You went underneath, you know, right on the trucks. You had to have a little board, about 18 inches long.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where you took it with you?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah, sure. Sometimes you'd find one in there, but not always. You could sit on it, you know.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: You know, you're kind of humped up.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: You never did that. No. I never have.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: A lot of my friends have done it. Yeah. They go from Portland to San Francisco on the freight.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: I hitchhiked a lot, but I never rode the freight. Gee, that's the only way. I never hitchhiked. I always had a Pack Sack, you know. And if you're going in, people don't like to pick you up, you know. Pack sackers, well, jumpers. So I never tried that hitchhiking. I tried it a couple times, but not very successful. So if I wanted to go in the place, I'd just jump in the freight and away I went.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have to watch out for the dicks?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: On some roads, the UP was bad, yeah, but in this country here, you take like the, well, the Milwaukee, the NP, the Great Northern, any of them, they were all good, you know. But the UP was always pretty hostile. I don't know why, but, well, they had a school where they educated people. They educated them where those folks know Cheyenne was. They would always write them freaks there, out of there, any direction. And they were pretty rough. It was for years on the yellow roads you could write for a dollar, a division, you know. Like from Spokane to Kootenay, you know, where Kootenay is, out of San Pointe to Pumala. That was the division then, that was a dollar. Kootenay to Paradise was another division, that was another dollar. Paradise to Missoula was another division, another dollar. Change crews, you see, that's when they had the old steamers, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you were riding a freight, you didn't pay nothing, right?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yes, pay a dollar. Oh, you did pay a dollar. Oftentimes, yeah. That's what I say, they had a dollar division. Otherwise, it'd tully off.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: But they cut that out, then you could write. Well, I've heard that, like, the breakies sometimes would hold guys up. Oh, yeah, sure. Sure they would.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: They used to hold up those firefighters to cut off them, you know. A whole bunch of them, all, Christ, maybe a hundred of them, you know, come off on a fire. They wouldn't have much. They'd be going this way, that way, east, west, or some way, and they'd write a freight. Spokane was kind of a jumping off place, you know, kind of a central, like, you know, for, well, for all the guys, they had to go in there to get them started, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But, what was I going to say now?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: So, they nailed firefighters. Oh, yeah, yeah, yeah, they'd rob them there, you know. If you had anything, they'd take it and leave you right, but if you had it hid out, planned it some place, and they found it, they'd tully off, as a rule.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you hide your money?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh, yeah, I hid your shoe, your sock, or your hat banded some place.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah, everybody tried to hide it some place, but them guys knew all the hiding places, you know. So, I've been robbed a hundred times.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's that, a lawnmower, or that's what it sounds like?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah, that's what that sounds like. That guy over there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: That big guy that lived there next door.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: There he is, there he is, oh yeah, he was buried about a month ago, I think. His wife, oh, she's a nice little bone.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Pretty little thing, huh?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: She's about forty, I guess. He was over a day, brought me over a few cakes. She works for Silver Dollar ten or five nights. She's been there now, I don't know, a couple of years. But they've lived there, 18 or 20 years, and that house, they own it. That big house. They got three kids, one's 18, the boy, the older boy, Jerry's 18, and the little girl is 16 or 17, and this kid that's mowing the lawn, he's about 15, I think. I think that's him mowing that lawn now.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: I told him he had time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he a lot older than her?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: No, he was 40. He was pretty young too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What killed him?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Paper set of hard and sad. That's pretty young to grow food. Yeah, he was an awful drinker, Jesus Christ. He had cirrhosis of the liver. They started to wallow with him, a couple of guys in the car. They took him out of the house over there, they had to help him. He was big and fat. And they got him in the car and he passed out when they was in Clarkson. Just crossing the bridge, you know. They took him to Christ's Aid. The doctor there looked at him and said, he can never make wallow wallow. Lucky he didn't die before morning, right here. And they treated him, done something of course, but he died that night.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: I was just a girl, but I didn't go out to the very ground. I just went to the chapel. Yeah. I had no bag. No, I didn't go out to the very ground. I rode with a guy in the car. I banged my pick up up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, that's why it ain't here, huh?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened to it? You banged up the app?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: One fender in the light. That ain't too bad. No, $260. That's bad. I got it in the shop now. I might get it today. I don't know. It's been there. We'd go last Monday. We'd go here too.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, you know, talking about booze. I was thinking about, you know, booze during Prohibition when it wasn't easy to get. I was thinking about that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where would you guys get it?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Any place. Any place at all that isn't pricey, but easy to find. If you knew, you know, if you weren't a stranger. A stranger would hide for a stranger. In fact, it was a pointer in the hotel. Geez, I remember when they sold it to Pierce right out of the gallon. That was 30, 31, during the depression. Good stuff. Pretty fair stuff. Some of it. Some of it wasn't worth a crap of damn. But, you know, I'd get pretty good stuff. I'd get it at the same place most of the time. $5. But then it went up and up and kept going up. I don't know what the hell it was when I legalized this whiskey. About $20 I guess for me. See, when this whiskey was legalized, 32. Something like that, yeah. Maybe 33. 33 I think, yeah. 33 I think. When Roosevelt got in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how often would guys go drinking?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: You know, the lumberjacks and that. Oh, uh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Being mostly weekends or drink?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh no, no, no, no. Not them days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: No, no. They'd stay three or four months.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In a camp?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Average, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They wouldn't drink in the camp?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh no.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Huh. No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why not?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, they just didn't want it I guess is all. If they did get drunk, they'd leave. Quit.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: No, they didn't drink much in camp. Very, very little. They do now though. They've all got bottles of, uh, roast up mass. They don't deserve, they don't get drunk though, you know. They take a couple of drinks maybe a day. That's it. Those that drink. And the old timers, they're all gone of course. Christ, there ain't none of them. I know.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: I look for them. Can't find them.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: I've looked for them. I can't find them. No, there ain't none.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Have you seen Bowles lately? Wallace Bowles?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: No, I've seen his sister not too many, not too long ago. He's got a younger sister.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah? Where'd she live?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Now she lives down in Bovill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Her husband died a couple of years ago and now she's moved down to Here?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Lewiston. Yeah. Well, he comes to Bovill and he was about eight years old. Wallace did. He was born in Michigan. Yeah, that's right. He joined the band. But he left when he was a kid. I haven't seen him for a couple of years. He's around. Oh yeah.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: You know, somebody was telling me that the IWW used to send out Flying squads sometimes, like if, say like on a train, if guys was getting held up, they might send out a flying squad and take care of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever remember anything like that?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh yeah, sure.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Sure. They used to be hijackers on these trains, you know. The fellas would be made abysms of it, you know. Them firefighters, their fellas especially, they have guns, you know. Jumping a boxcar, you wouldn't know where the words of the train gun would go. They'd be usually two of them, you know. They'd first come and run over on the other end of the car, go with the tape first and kept the others over there until they called them out, you know. They'd be two of them, you know. They'd have guns and they'd shoot you. They shot quite a few guys. Show them off.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: If you put up a fight, you know. Oh yeah. That's pretty tough stuff.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah, but they never looked for nobody, you know. They didn't care much, you know. The authorities, they'd find the body and put a little note in the paper about it and that's all they would ever do to it. Unless they happened to, unless them guys held up someone in town or a bank or something, then they'd just catch them. Yeah, if it was a working step, they didn't... No! Hell yeah. They wouldn't give a goddamn.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, if you was held up, did you ever put up a fight?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh no.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: It wouldn't be... I got stuck up right in front of this shack. You're kidding. Yes. Twenty-six dollars I had. Oh shit. I always turn the force around when I go over telling the lights, you know. But I didn't this night and I was coming home and a guy was on the porch. And he stopped and started talking to me. I didn't think nothing of it, you know, until he asked me if I had any money. I didn't answer him then. Then there was two other guys coming from over here, you know. I see the three of them and they just said they'd give us your money. So I gave them the bill. I said, give me back the bill. So they did. They took the money. That's all there was to it. I never even mentioned it, you know, for two weeks afterwards. I happened to tell somebody about it. But there's no use of... I wouldn't know if I'd see them right there. It was dark and they had whiskers.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: How come... You know, like on the trains, I would think that maybe, you know, the men would try to get together for self-defense, you know, protect themselves against that kind of thing. Well, there wasn't much chance.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: They were mostly all individuals, you know. They'd see that gun and then think of themselves.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: In the old days, it was so colorful. I mean, it just seemed really exciting. You'd say it looks like way now. It does now. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't it look that way then?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Not to me. It was just another day's work. I didn't think it was too colorful. I always liked horses. I always had a good team of horses. Took good care of them.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: But these old cats, I don't care much for them. They're just an old piece of machinery.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Make a lot of noise too. Oh yeah.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, you know, one thing I was thinking about when you're traveling on a train like that is jungling up and that kind of thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the facilities like for jungling up?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, some of them, like these Lumberjack towns, they all had pretty good jungles, you know. They were frying pans and getting cans and dishes and everything. They were kept in shape. They were always washed and hanging up, everything was, you know. The jungle was a pretty likely spot, right by a nice spring or something. But to get out on the main line, of course, everybody jumbled up then pretty much, you know. Everybody. Stips of all kinds, you know. But it got so you had to carry your own riggin', you know. Frying pan. You could always find a coffee can, but you had to have plates and a frying pan, you know. Tin plate and frying pan, that's all you needed. Little Chuck. Yeah, you jumbled up every place you went, you know. Well, this is the rule, you were traveling on goddamn short money. And you had to make it last till you got where you were going. If you were traveling through a strange country, you know, where you weren't acquainted or didn't know anybody or get out of this Lumberjack area, you know, and you had pretty tough going. As long as you was around these Lumberjacks, there was nothing to it, you know. Always get something, you know. But you get out in these, get away from this area, you know. Pretty binding sometimes, you had to get right out and rustle.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How'd you rustle?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, any way you could get it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Does that mean like you steal chickens and that sort of thing?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh yes.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh yeah. That got to be a pretty serious offense.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Really? Steal chickens?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah. Oh yeah, I don't know why, I think it's five years in this state now.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well that's pretty tough, that's pretty high for about a dollar, a stewing hand, it probably worth about a buck.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah, I never, I've stole lots of them, but I never got caught. But I know some that did.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh yeah, right in the chicken coopers. Serious. We used to catch them on a fish hook. If they come around the jungles, you know, wherever we were jungling up, they would sometimes don't know what anyone was around there. Close to a farm or something. Put a worm on a hook and they'll bite her, you know, and you can hook them. Lead them right to you. They won't pull much.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah, that's probably better.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well what about, what about bumming grub? Can you go like, into stores and ask them for?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh yeah, butcher shops are always good. I don't know why they'd be, they wouldn't be now, you know, because you can't see the butcher. You know, the butcher always worked out of the box, you know, cutting meat and you'd be bumming me all year or something. Might not be the best, but it'd give you something, you know. Yeah, a chunk of bacon or something like that. But I don't know how many hell you'd go at it now.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Everything is wrapped in. Food stamps is the way you gotta do it now. Yeah, that's right, but I never thought of that. That's about the way it is now. Well I don't get nothing like that, I just get my attention is all. I never tried to get anything like that, I don't know. Well if you've got enough to get by, well you know, that's the main thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, but them food stamps, that's quite a racket, isn't it?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well I think for some people it is. You know, you get these college kids that are on it, that kind of thing. Some have been, you know, I've known it to be the case. I don't know if it's that way now. You know, there's always a certain amount of that kind of thing, but then there are people that really do need them.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh yes, sure. I see some in the store here. The only time I've seen them, they're an awful handicap.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They're through the checkout girl and everybody else, don't they?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Giving them change, and some will get cigarettes. I see you can't get cigarettes. Yeah, well sure, that ain't food. Can't eat them. I see one with a carton of cigarettes and they made her, the checkout girl took them. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you know, if you were getting grub like that, would you, would one guy get it for a whole bunch, you know, for say five, six guys? Would everybody go Russell for himself?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh, that's great.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well you would stake out one guy, go after one other, go after another, go after another, and so on. Coffee was the hardest to get, you had to buy that. But you could get a meal, stew, meat, and all that, you know. I couldn't live very long on it, but why don't guys that come into town, well like Sandpoint or something like that, and stay a week, two weeks sometimes. Just bumming.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Bumming off the lumberjacks?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Anybody on the street or any god damn place. And get drunk too. Yes, sir. Oh, lumberjack, he was always good for whatever he had.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: That your car out there?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, that's my...

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: The same one you had?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well was there different kinds of guys then? I mean you say it wasn't just the lumberjacks, but there were guys that were real, real full time hobos on the rails too that you spent the time with?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh yes, sure, sure, sure. So they did move from town to town. And they always had a racket, a lot of them had a racket of some kind, they were selling something or some god damn... Yeah, fountain pens, just to get the conversation started with the person so they could bum it, you know. Yeah, sure, there was all kinds of that.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: They'd pick up anything that was loose too, but they didn't have guts enough to hold anybody up, you know, they just had to get by that way. And they could glue work, some of them worked, once in a while they'd take on the labor.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: But I don't know, I haven't... the last freight train I rode was just before the war, I was in 41 I think. I just made a short trip and I don't know who's riding them now or if there's anybody riding them. I don't know that, but I got a... I hear these guys, these construction workers talk about Pasco. Pasco used to be an awful place, you know, for bums going through, you know. Yeah, just going boat wage, you know. Lots of guys. Oh Jesus Christ, 100, 200 on the train. But, well, it's long, I've been there 40 years I guess, but I don't know what the hell's going on there now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you meet many guys you knew on them trains?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Guys like myself, you know. Yeah, working in the woods. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could you trust the guys on the train?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh yeah, sure. I've heard that you just about had to have a wobbly car to ride. Yeah, you did.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah, if you didn't have they'd dump you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that the truth? You'd either have one or you'd get off the train?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah, jump.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they ask you to see your car?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: You see, it was... A lot of guys didn't want to show it because it was a year for carrying a wobbly car. No kidding. Yeah, in California, Oregon, Idaho. One year. You see, that was a revolutionary organization. Yeah. They believed in the overthrow of the government by force. Yeah. I lost my car to the old one. Oh boy. 36th I think.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: I'll tell you, I've heard more good, you know, about that union. Oh yeah. I mean that's all I hear about from the working man. Yeah.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: It was good. You didn't have the... there was no centralized authority like there is in these big corporations, big unions. You know, like... well, you can't hold... if you're working in a camp here now and you belong to the CIO or the IWA, it's the same. You can't just hold a meeting in the camp and take a strike vote. You can, but it don't mean nothing. You can't strike. It'd be illegal. You've got to... you've got to go... it's got to go to the executive committee finally before it'll be sanctioned, you know. And that might be your crisis. But the robberies, you know, you could... a quorum, if you had enough for a quorum, you could hold a meeting and call a strike and it was legal, you know, with the organization. It was illegal with the government and everything else but the organization. And you could go out and get what you wanted. Or get nothing. It depends on how much power you had.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did, like, did you guys sing them songs very much? The wobbly songs?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah. I used to have a book, or I had several of them, but I used to know some of them songs, but I don't know them anymore.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Like Pie in the Sky?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Hallelujah on the Bum?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The Commonwealth of the Toil?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh, there was lots of them. Joe Hill.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah, I used to know some of them but I forgot them then. Oh, we used to sell them shit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Guys in the camp, like in the jungles, would they sing them?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Sure, any place. Camp, any place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But, like, these guys on the trains, a bunch of them weren't lumberjacks, but they were wobbly's too, huh?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They was in the Union?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah, they, the wobbly's covered different industries, you know, the same as these organizations do now. They, well, the construction workers and lumberjacks, sheet metal workers, the like of that, they covered a lot of different industries, you know. They were pretty strong at one time, headquarters in Chicago.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, I heard Spokane was a western headquarters. It was, yeah. They were always hanging out. Yeah.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: I don't know if there's nothing there now. I don't think so. I wish there was. I'd like to... Yeah, you could get a lot of dopers. You could meet some of them old fellas.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they, uh, uh, what did they believe about sabotage?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: I've seen the Sab Cat, you know, the Black Cat and the Booped. But, uh, did they, you know, like I've heard a lot of people say that all this, an awful lot of things that was pinned on them they'd never done.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But didn't they believe in sabotage when, at times, when it was necessary?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, it was never sanctioned in French, you know, but it was a part of the game. If it was necessary, uh, they, uh, they used to do things like, uh, well, if they got thrown off in a freight train or something, the next thing they'd do is be, uh, throw sand in the journal box of another train, you see. Call it a hot box, you know. Had to be set out and stipe, otherwise it'd sketch the whole box guard part, which just happened to you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean you throw it in the journal box?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah. Just a handful of sand.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah. Well, there was, I never knew of any Wobblies doing it, but I've known of other guys doing it that got thrown off, you know. Well, the next chance they got, they even went to the sand. Wouldn't be that train, probably, but it'd be another one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about in the woods?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Like, uh, I know during strikes, you know, the companies always used to say that they, you know, they stuck, uh, oh, stick a spike in a log. Oh, yeah.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: And it goes through the saw. Yeah. I've heard of it being done. I never did it, of course, myself. I never did commit no act of sabotage. I didn't believe in it. But, uh, well, the company did it themselves, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The company did it and blamed it on the Wobs?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah, sure.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah. You bet they did.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: You know, you was telling me about that, that, um, 36th strike that he was in, and you tell me about that, um, you know, the time when they killed a couple guys in near Pierce.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that Fromalt's camp? Or was that somewhere else?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: It was Fromalt's crew.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: See, Fromalt was, that company forced him to start Operation Jiffy. Uh, the time breaks strike, you know. And they, uh, he had a contract with them. There was nothing in it about labor trouble or anything. Just a written contract, you know. And they made him start up. That's when the whole trouble started. And then, some of his crew, like, these real loyal hams, why, they undertook the doodle shooting, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did, did, did you guys try to stop them from working?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah. We had a picket line.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did any of his guys get beaten?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah, they got beat up, but, uh, more or less individually, you know. Not that night they didn't know, but they got it afterwards in town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, they got it afterwards in, uh, oh, they didn't get beat up before the shooting started?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: No.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: So, that's how that happened. And then we, of course, they shot through the wind chaffes, too, you know. And, uh, had to stop the truck. We all bailed off, some words, you know. Cut off and got to hell. Any place you could. We didn't have a thing, you know. Not even a TV stock. I got down behind the duels.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: You know, this guy, one guy who was in Fromel, Fromel's camp, and I think it was a strike breaker, told me that, uh, that, um, that action started after the guys on the picket line beat up two of the guys in the camp. That's what he, that's what he said. No. They'd done that.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, there might have been some, uh, mix ups, you know, before that in town. There was always two or three fights in town every day, you know. Pierce. But, uh, maybe more, but, uh, I, oh, I know all them fights, but I don't, can't connect them with anything with any of the shooting at all.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: And they really shot, they killed a couple of guys. Oh, yeah.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh, sure. There was nine rifles in that group.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: You know, you was telling me that, that, you know, how you got away and then they, and then they put you in jail here. And I was thinking about that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean, did, uh, did you, were you really pissed off about that whole thing?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: I mean, if I was doing a year, a year's time, I don't think I'd like it very much for something like that. I mean, you didn't really do nothing.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Because you got more.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah, the, well, the country was hostile too, you know, the courts and the law and everything, you know. It was pretty hard to, if you got pinched, it was pretty hard to beat any kind of a rap at all in front of these judges, you know. We had no lawyer from Spokane, I never met him, but he defended the rest of this bunch that I was with. He didn't defend me, I had another one, but his name was Don, you might as well, I might as well go get up there and talk to you, you know, with him.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, it seems like they should have done better than that. They should have tried to get a decent lawyer. Well, they didn't have the money.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: We had never had a strike fund, you know, they had never, never had a strike fund. Not like these now, Christ Almighty, a million dollars in that strike fund, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't know, they have more good workers there, aren't they?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Jesus Christ, I don't know how much of a strike fund they got. They've been saving it up for a long time. Oh yes, and they're taxed every month.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How come you didn't take their lawyer, but you got your own? Did you think you could do better or what?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, yes, I thought I could do better.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you, or did they all get the same thing as you?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: No, there was five, got a year and the rest. Just minor sentences, you know, 30 days and something like that. See, there was about nine of us all together in that deal. But they picked some and made, oh I don't know, ringleaders I guess they called them. I was no ringleader, I was just an ordinary gift, you know, working gift. But I happened to get caught in them under the circumstances and that's how that happened.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So they made you a ringleader?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, I, um...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They called you one?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah, I was just a striker is all I was, you know. I was on the strike committees and like a vet, you know, I took an active part in the organization but never nothing violent, you know, I didn't go overboard over it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what was it like in jail for a year?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh, good, good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it pretty good?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: That old dent was Sheriff. Harry Dent, please name. Yeah, we had three newspapers every day, Spokane, Oregon paper and this paper here. And they could bring anything in they wanted to, you know. And I had a lot of friends. I had, oh Christ, snuff, tobacco, money and everything. I wasn't hurting any but, uh...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could you drink in there?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, once in a while you could get it in, that was the problem, you know. They used to first come and visit you, you know. If they came in contact, it was like you and me sitting here, you know. If you come in way close to them, they would have had the first to see. No, they didn't go for... Oh, I brought a couple of bottles up the back way with a rope or a string, you know. Yeah. But, uh, well, there was a big bunch in there and then pass it around, it's all gone. You couldn't... There was no chance to get a go on or nothing, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many guys was in one cell there?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, there was four.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Four?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah, they brought a bunch of prisoners up from Leavenworth. They were federal prisoners, you know. And they was working on a road job at Kooskiaa. They were all one-year men. They got mostly white slavery charges. They were from all over the United States, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's white slavery mean?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, transporting a woman from one state to another for prostitution. Yeah, that's white slavery. That's a year minimum, you see. Well, that's what these guys were doing. One year, most of them. And they had nothing, you know. They were from... Well, they weren't like me right from here, you see. They were from all over, you know. And, of course, they had better connections than I did, but they weren't where they could get to them or anything, you know. And they had nothing. So we kept them in tobacco and razor blades and the like of that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was they being worked during the day?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah, they were. No, they was in there for about a week and then they took them to Kooskia. And I don't know where in hell they kept them there.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: But you didn't have to work or nothing. You just sat there and waited your time.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Did you get out on parole or did you serve a whole... No, I served 11 months over.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah. Then, though, it's that... You know, what really got me when you was talking about it before was the blackballing afterwards. Oh, yeah. That really got me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was wondering how the hell did you make a living during those years? I mean, how did you get by when every place you went, they'd kick you out? Did you change your name or anything?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: No.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: I was born June 1, you see, that Social Security come out of my house. I think about that time, you know. I think that fall, I was working in California, you know, Martin, Princeton. But you got that name and number and everything, you know, and I didn't want to change. I said, to hell with it, I'll keep my own name. So when I went to work in a logging camp, then I would maybe get in 10 days and they'd come and tell me, you know, that I had to go.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: I mean, this went on for, you know, enough, long enough time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's what I was wondering is how did you make a living during that time?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, you just work when you couldn't, when you couldn't, why you didn't. Just make it some other way. Go to work for a farmer, anybody.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you do that, work for farmers and stuff?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: I worked for a couple of them.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: A couple of them, yeah.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Mm-hmm. I worked for Yakima one time, Toppenish I think it was, at Yakima Valley. Ten cents an hour, ten hours a day.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Pitching out the fella. Jesus, that's hard work. Yeah. And then, you know, stone boats, I call them stone boats, you know, you've seen them. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A buck a day, ain't it?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Ten hours. That's nothing compared to what you was making as a lumberjack. Oh no, no, but you had to live, you know, and I don't like bumming or anything like that. I don't know, but I would if I had to, I did, but it didn't appeal to me. Soliciting arms.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could the union support you at all? I mean, could you go to them for money?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah, they offered me money, but I wouldn't take it. I told them to hell with you, I can make my own way. Yeah, they offered me money, they offered me money when I got out of jail. And they offered me money when I was in jail, they come up to see me and they represent, there's some guys I didn't know to all come up. There was someone there every day, but I never took no money or nothing from them. They had an office there, a hall in Spokane then, you know, on Bernard Street between Trent and Main. That was there, it was. When I got out of jail, I was going through Spokane, I met a guy and he said, come on down to the hall. But I had money then, I got money when I got out of Pierce. I went to Pierce and got a couple hundred dollars. So I went down to the hall and they wanted to get me money there, but I didn't take it. They didn't have much anyway, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that money at Pierce that you borrowed from somebody?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah, individual.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Friend?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Businessman. That's pretty good, they just gave you a couple hundred bucks. Yeah, they probably figured, they never figured on seeing me again, you know.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: I said, where are you going?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: I said, I don't know, I'm going to get out of here. He said, I'll take it sometime. I sent it to them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it really hot for you around here after you got out of the jail?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh no.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Around Pierce?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But they just wouldn't hire you?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: No. I never tried there, you see, I never tried to go to work there. Because I knew they wouldn't put me to work. Then they opened an office in Bovill, you know, over there, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, the union did?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: The company, yeah, right. The hiring hall. And I heard guys say they couldn't go to work here. I never was in this place up here then, you know. In Orofino, they just opened that. I never went in there at all. I went in there in 1941, the first time. But I never went in there after the strike. I heard guys say they'd been in there and they wouldn't hire them. They said they were all filled up. Well, I'm going over to Bovill, I'll try that place, I'll get out of there. I thought to myself, you play hell getting out of there too. There was about a hundred of us, I guess, all together, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Guys that couldn't get work because they were Wobblies?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: I seen the list. Guys showed up to be in Oregon.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was they all from, mostly from around here?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: All from around here.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, that is, they were, they worked around here. They were just like myself. They were from wherever they were, that's where they belonged, you know, at the time.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: I went down to Oregon and I went to work for a guy who used to work for a marble court here.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: And his name was Ed Gaffney. He used to be superintendent on marble, a walking boss. I went down there, he was working for the Spring River Lumber Company. Hell, I went right to work. As soon as he seen me, he put me to work. I worked about ten days. The clerk come in one night and he said, Mr. Gaffney, I went to see you over in his house. He lived in the camp, in the house, you know, he had his own house. He was married to course he was. He and his wife. I knew what it was. I packed my sack right away and I didn't go over. I was going to leave in the morning, you see, I knew what it was. But the clerk come again, he said he wanted to see me, so I went over then, you know. That was about an hour later and he told me, he showed me the list. I was fourth or fifth from the top. He said, I was... An honor. He said, you've got to go, Bill.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: So I did come into Commonwealth Falls and I met a couple guys there that I knew real well, you know. And they were from around, they used to work around here. They were black balls too, you see. But they knew it, they were from around the world, you know. I told them, I said, I just worked for Daphne up in the Earth's Big River for... I got in ten days, I said, you might get ten days too. If you want to try it, that's where they were heading for, you see. So they did, they went up and he gave them ten days' worth. I gave them a little money, I gave them, I had forty, fifty dollars I guess. But, uh, they got ten days' worth.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you go around mostly by yourself in them days or did you travel with friends?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh, it depends.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: If I met up with a guy I liked, you know, I... And he was going my way, why we'd go together. Otherwise, I went alone with him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that sour you on the Union?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: No. No. I, uh, I don't know, I was expecting all of that, you know. I knew that the Union was outlawed, you know what I mean. I knew it was illegal under the present set up, you know. I knew that, of course, and I was expecting that, you know. If we were winners, you see, it would be different. But we didn't come out winners. And they, they wouldn't allow us to win, you know. They wanted to kill the organization, you see, which they did. That was the last feeble attempt they ever made.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: The IWW.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you figure at the time that the chances were pretty bleak for it to work, chances weren't too good? I mean, did you figure that in the 36? The odds being against you?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: What, uh... No, the odds, it seems to me, when even in 36 you must have known the odds weren't too great. I mean, with all the force of the government and the company working against the Union.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean to win the strike?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh, uh, I kind of thought we might win at first. Yeah, we come out 100%. You mean, uh, everybody, everything shut down. Oh, bang, they run out of logs at the mill. The mill didn't go out with us, you know, it was different, you know. The mill never did go with the Woods and the IWW strike, you know. They were always, they stay away from that mill, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How come?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, the mill was all married men and they had homes and they had...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Too much to lose, huh?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: ...commitments they had to make, you know, all this and that. So they probably said, well, they were all single men, didn't give a goddamn for nothing, you know. They always steered away from the mill, you know. But the mill wasn't in with us this time, but, uh... And this last IWW strike... But they run out of timber out here, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They had to shut down, didn't they?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: And they had to shut down. But that's when the pressure come on us, you see. They started that shooting match up there. Then they declared martial law and brought the National Guardians, Orpino and Pierce. And that was on the way out then.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: But you expected it anyway, but I mean, it was, you figured it was, it was the right thing to do for the men to try to do. Yeah.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: So you really had to do it. I thought it was right, yeah. I knew that they were going to try to prove us wrong, but the camps themselves, you know, was in awful shape. Lousy, dirty. And you, when you went to work in the spring, if you stayed there all summer, you had the same blankets on that bed when you left in the fall. And no change of sheets or nothing, you know. Oh, God.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: I thought that was what the 17-8 strike was together. Well, it was. It was on the coast.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: But, uh...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They just got back to work after the depression right then, hadn't they?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: I mean, they were just starting up again in a couple of years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean the, these around here?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, 36 was just after the... Yeah, yeah, yeah. That's right. They was just getting the swing of the motion. Well, those had been in one term and...

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: They really didn't do a thing for the conditions of the camps. Oh, no, no.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: But during the strike, they worked in headquarters. The governor had come, you know, Ben Ross was governor. Yeah, right. He come and then I went as far as headquarters with him. And I wanted him to go a little further, you know, but he wouldn't. Well, there were some camps there. They had no floors in the bunkhouse, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you with him? Were you with him?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Or showing him the camps and stuff?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did he say about it? Is that anything?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: The only place he went was headquarters. And then they'd done some work that had sealed it up, you know, off, you see. And painted them inside during that first month we were on strike. And that's the only place that Bradbury would go or anybody... I said, come on down the line, governor. I said, I'll show you some camps. Well, he said, I ain't got time now and I think this looks very, very good here. And that's what he said. Then back to Pierce we went and that was the end of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, were there a lot of people with him or just a few guys?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh, no, he had an aide, a military man he was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you the union representative that went with him?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah. You're the guy they sent to... Yes. There were three of us.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Three union guys.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The company has some guys there too?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: And then the two or three with the governor. There was one guy in uniform, I remember him. His name was Abendroth. He was a captain or a colonel. I think he was a captain. National Guard man. An aide he was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But the a government had his own car and everything, you know what I mean?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Driver.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you think he came up with an open mind?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: I mean, he probably...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think he knew just what he wanted to see?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: No, I don't think he had an open mind. He spread his story. W .W. Same as all them people were, you know. They couldn't afford to let that organization win, you know. Jesus Christ. They would get stronger, then spread and holy Jesus, they'd have a love of diamonds. So they had to squash it, you know, at the earliest possible moment. Gear out.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Was it pretty strong in the... like between the 17 strike and the 36 strike, was it...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a pretty strong presence all the way through that period of time?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh no, no. There was...

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: we take from 1923 on... Well, that's when the Wobblies started to die on the coast. They were stronger on the coast, you know, they were... That's when they started to die. Twenty-three.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: I don't know what the hell happened. They just... Faded, you know. Except some old die-hard, they never would die. They kept going, but...

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh, they couldn't let that out to go on, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were most of your friends among the nine guys that got nailed? Were them guys that had worked around here on the Clearwater for a long time?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Some of them were straight old, small hands, you know. They worked all the time for him.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: But... Well, I know them, you know. Worked with some of them, they used to... But I don't know how they happened to pick that particular bunch of... Well, actually, what I meant was them guys on the Wobblies side.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: I mean, your friends, the guys that were... That's the guys I'm talking about.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about them? Oh, were they guys that had been working in here for a long time?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: They were all local men then. They'd been here for a while. Yeah, they'd been here for a while.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Did they deny... I don't know, how many guys did you say that it was on... Who were the Wobbly leaders that they... The guys that shut the camps down, I mean...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean the leaders?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah, how many of...

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well... Oh, I don't know. Just how many there was. Must have been quite a few, though, to shut down all the camps.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh, you mean men?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah, the men. Oh, there was 3 ,000.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, I figured that them that was kind of taking the lead, I mean, somebody had to sell them on the... Yeah, there was probably...

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh, the real leaders, there was probably a dozen. You know. That is the real active members and the guys that could get up and talk, you know, at a meeting or talk to anybody, you know. They were probably a dozen of them there. They were really good men, you know. But they were sincere, you know. Yeah, they thought they... They didn't care, you know, about their own selves if they could win. That's... You gain a point.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was it their idea or did the idea of the strike come out of Spokane to make that strike in 36?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Well, uh...

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: I don't know. There was a lot of strangers coming here and went to work. Wobblies. They were really the start of it, you see. You know, they kicked about everything and got everybody else all fuzzed up and kicking them. Finally, the lid flew off, you know.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Yeah, that's the way that happened.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were them guys, you think they was from Spokane or think they were sent out to do it?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Oh, no, they weren't sent out. They were just... Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about them CCCs during that time? Did you ever have much to do with all them young kids that they sent out from the east or the city?

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: No, I worked in a CC camp. I was working for the Panhandle Lumber Company down on the Monterey River. And I stayed in a CC camp for a month sometimes. We were building bridges for them. There was 200 of them in that camp, all young lads, you know. Chicago and, oh, I don't know where the hell they were all from. But they were an awful bunch of bastards. I think they got a dollar a day, something like that. We wasn't getting a hell of a lot more than that. We was getting about $3 .40, I think, working for the Panhandle.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they tough kids?

The push would come in and tell you, I need a teamster and I need two teamsters or I need swampers or come on all you guys and sign up. Or maybe you might say I'm all filled up, morning boy. Come around again, but you could always go back and stay overnight, eat supper, stay overnight, breakfast, bum the cook for a chunk of bacon and away you go again.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell

words: You know, I've heard a lot of guys talking about blowing in. And I've wondered about that a lot. Now, I'm often wondered about that. Why, say, a guy would be, you know, you kind of have to buy drinks for everybody and spend it all. A guy wouldn't say, try to save maybe twenty-five bucks or something like that if you have a stake.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: Most of them, I never tried to save nothing. I always borrowed when I left town. I'd go to my landlord or landlady or somebody and I'd borrow. Not to get out. Or maybe jump on a freight and go. That was...

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: So I guess you just wasn't worried about making more money then. You knew that money was there to be made. Yeah, sure, sure.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: They run winter camps around here too, you know. You could always go up to the first river and go to work. Always. Christ, there was twenty-five camps out of the first river.

speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
words: With a hundred and fifty men, each one fell to the sea. In front of the... well, they'd... They'd slave haul, you know. They'd haul them on slaves and dump them in the river and drive them in the spring. That's the way they got them for meals.

Maybe if you wanted to go and drive someplace else, you'd only work a few days on that drive and bunch here and go to wherever you wanted to go, you know. I just like marbles. All tramps, you know, there. The drive you hadn't seen for a year. Same with the Coeur d 'Alene River, same thing. This drive here hadn't started yet, you know. speaker: William (Michigan Bill) Stowell
